claim: Context engineering is the challenge of feeding an LLM exactly the right context for the next step in an agentic workflow - it emerged as a buzzword because many people independently discovered it was the core difficulty.
actionable: In every agent loop, audit what context is necessary for that step; actively choose what context to include, exclude, or externalize.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Naive agent implementations naively plumb all tool-call context back into the model, causing token explosion and context window hits; typical production agents use hundreds of tool calls.
actionable: Never naively return full tool-call outputs; implement context management from day one - expect ~50-100x token overhead if you don't.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Context rot is a real degradation in LLM performance with increasing context length independent of whether context is cached - caching solves cost/latency, not quality.
actionable: Use caching for cost optimization, but implement context engineering (offloading, compression, isolation) to maintain output quality at scale.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Offload context to external systems like filesystems or agent state rather than keeping all tool call outputs in the message history - write summaries instead.
actionable: Externalize tool outputs to disk/database; store only a summary or pointer in the message history; fetch full context on demand.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Summarization is critical but non-trivial - use careful prompting or fine-tuned models to ensure summaries capture high-recall key points while compressing significantly.
actionable: If summarizing, spend effort on recall - prompt the model to list all key bullet points exhaustively; test that summaries let the LLM know whether to retrieve full context.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Multi-agent systems work well when agents do read-only context gathering in parallel and a single agent does all writing at the end - not when each sub-agent makes conflicting write decisions.
actionable: For deep research/analysis, use parallel read sub-agents feeding one central writer; for coding with conflicting decisions, use single-agent with subdivided tasks instead.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Agentic retrieval using simple file tools and LLM.txt often outperforms complex RAG pipelines with vector indexing and semantic search for code and documentation retrieval.
actionable: Start with agentic search (file tools + good descriptions) before building vector stores; use LLM-generated descriptions in index files for better semantic understanding.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Caching improves latency and cost but doesn't solve long-context problems - context rot applies whether the context is cached or not.
actionable: Use implicit caching (Anthropic/Gemini) automatically; don't rely on caching alone for context quality - still apply context engineering practices.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Prompt engineering and MCP servers should include explicit prompts showing agents how to use the server - the 'how to use' instruction should live in the server, not just documentation.
actionable: When creating an MCP server, embed a usage prompt in the server definition itself; don't rely on external documentation.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: The bitter lesson applied to AI engineering: add structure when necessary for current model capabilities, but continuously reassess and remove structure as models improve to avoid bottlenecking.
actionable: Review your system design quarterly; if models improved but you haven't simplified architecture, you're likely leaving wins on the table.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Tool calling reliability has dramatically improved (from 2024 to 2025); systems built with workarounds to avoid tool calling are now bottlenecked by their own workarounds.
actionable: If you built agent fallbacks or non-tool-call alternatives in 2024, audit them in 2025; remove workarounds that are now dead code.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Context poisoning is a failure mode where LLM hallucinations in agent history steer the agent off track; keep failures in context for correction, but be aware of this risk.
actionable: Monitor for cascading hallucinations in long agent runs; implement checkpoints where a human can reset context if the agent is tracking a false assumption.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Context engineering has five main categories: offloading, context isolation, retrieval, reducing context, and caching - they address different layers of the problem.
actionable: Use this five-category taxonomy to audit your agent implementation; ensure you're addressing at least 2-3 categories depending on your use case.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Low-level orchestration frameworks (nodes, edges, state) like LangGraph are aligned with the bitter lesson; high-level agent abstractions that hide implementation are problematic because you can't easily tear them down.
actionable: Choose low-level frameworks you can reassemble; avoid high-level 'import Agent' abstractions that lock you into one design pattern.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Memory systems pair well with human-in-the-loop workflows where user feedback is captured and reflected back into system instructions over time.
actionable: When building ambient agents, capture user edits to tool calls as feedback; use LLMs to reflect on these changes and update system prompts automatically.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain

claim: Open deep research outperforms commercial systems by using parallelized read agents for research with a single write agent at the end - it demonstrates bitter lesson principles in practice.
actionable: For research-heavy workflows, replicate the parallel-read + single-write pattern; it scales better than linear agent loops.
source_title: Context Engineering for Agents - Lance Martin, LangChain
